title: Atrial Flutter
oneLiner: A supraventricular macro-reentrant tachycardia with a characteristic “sawtooth” atrial rhythm (~300 bpm), causing symptoms and stroke risk, especially in older adults with structural heart disease.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette**
You are called to see a 72-year-old man with known hypertension and mild COPD. He is in the surgical ward post-hip replacement. The nurses charted his pulse at 135 bpm, BP 105/60, SpO₂ 96% on air. He feels “fluttering in my chest” and slightly short of breath. ECG shows narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm with no clear P waves — but on lead II you spot a subtle “sawtooth” pattern. After carotid sinus massage (or adenosine 6 mg IV) the ventricular rate slows transiently and the sawtooth waves become obvious.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues and what they tell you**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Age >65, hypertension, COPD | Structural heart disease and atrial stretch predispose to reentry circuits |
| Post-operative setting | Increased sympathetic tone, fluid shifts, electrolyte changes trigger arrhythmia |
| Heart rate 135–150 bpm | This is around 2:1 AV block — typical flutter rate ~300, so ventricular rate is half |
| Narrow QRS tachycardia | Supraventricular origin; broad QRS would suggest VT or pre-excited |
| Sawtooth waves on lead II / III / aVF | The classic “flutter waves” — continuous, biphasic, best seen in inferior leads |
| Carotid sinus massage / adenosine transiently slows rate | AV nodal blockade reveals underlying flutter waves without terminating the arrhythmia |
| Mild SOB, palpitations, borderline BP | Hemodynamic compromise is often less severe than with AF at same ventricular rate, but can be significant |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**

- **Variable AV block** — The patient may have an irregularly irregular pulse (like AF) if the block changes from 2:1 to 3:1 or 4:1. Don’t be fooled: look for flutter waves buried in the T waves.
- **Broad QRS tachycardia** — If pre-existing bundle branch block or rate-related aberrancy, the QRS can be wide. Mistaking this for ventricular tachycardia is a classic error. Clues: irregularity, sawtooth waves, adenosine response.
- **Silent / isolated stroke** — The first manifestation may be an embolic stroke. ECG shows flutter in sinus rhythm? No — you must maintain high suspicion in any elderly stroke patient. Ask about palpitations.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition**
Atrial flutter is a fast, regular rhythm originating in the atria due to a single macro-reentrant circuit. The atria beat at about 300 times per minute, but the AV node can only pass through a fraction of those impulses, typically every second (2:1 block) or third (3:1 block), giving a ventricular rate of 150 or 100 bpm.

**Why it matters clinically**

- **Frequency** — Less common than AF (about 1/10th as common) but still encountered regularly in acute medicine, cardiology, and perioperative care.
- **Stakes** — Atrial flutter carries a stroke risk almost as high as AF (though some studies suggest slightly lower). Anticoagulation is mandatory for most patients.
- **Pitfalls** — Easily missed on ECG if you don’t look for sawtooth waves. Easily treated but often recurs without definitive therapy (ablation). Mismanagement of ventricular rate can cause heart failure.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story**
Atrial flutter is a reentrant tachycardia. In typical (isthmus-dependent) flutter, the circuit runs around the tricuspid annulus, using the cavotricuspid isthmus as a critical slow conduction zone. The wavefront travels up the right atrial free wall, down the interatrial septum, and back through the isthmus. This loop takes about 200 ms, giving an atrial cycle length of ~200 ms → atrial rate ~300/min.

**Why each key sign happens**

- **Sawtooth waves on ECG** — The continuous, undulating baseline (best seen in II, III, aVF) reflects atrial depolarisation without an isoelectric interval. The circuit produces a constant direction of activation that creates biphasic deflections.
- **Ventricular rate ~150 bpm** — The AV node cannot conduct 1:1 at 300 bpm (its effective refractory period is too long). In a healthy heart, 2:1 block is the default. Higher degrees (3:1, 4:1) occur with AV nodal disease or drugs.
- **Irregular pulse if variable block** — Changes in autonomic tone (breathing, walking, pain) alter AV node refractoriness, causing the block ratio to vary and making the rhythm irregular.
- **Carotid sinus massage shows flutter** — Vagal manoeuvres prolong AV nodal refractoriness, increasing block to 3:1 or 4:1 without affecting the atrial circuit. The sawtooth becomes visible because fewer QRS complexes obscure the baseline.
- **Adenosine transiently slows but does not terminate** — Adenosine blocks AV node conduction but the atrial reentry circuit is not dependent on AV node. After adenosine wears off (seconds), the atrial rate returns. This distinguishes flutter from AVNRT.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition**
The post-operative patient has a hyperadrenergic state → decreased AV node refractoriness → 2:1 block → ventricular rate 150. Baseline structural changes (atrial stretch from hypertension) maintain the substrate for reentry.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Structural heart disease** (most common) | Hypertension, coronary artery disease, valvular heart disease (especially mitral valve), cardiomyopathy | Older patient with known IHD or HF; left atrial enlargement on echo |
| **Post-cardiac surgery** | Especially after CABG or valve surgery — atrial incisions or ischemia create substrate | Recent sternotomy, drains, chest tubes |
| **Pulmonary causes** | COPD, pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, sleep apnoea | Hypoxia, CO₂ retention, right heart strain |
| **Endocrine / metabolic** | Hyperthyroidism (especially in elderly), electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) | Weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance; or diuretic use, low K⁺ |
| **Toxic / drug-induced** | Alcohol (“holiday heart”), caffeine, sympathomimetics, digoxin toxicity (less common) | Binge drinking, new stimulant medication |
| **Idiopathic** | Lone atrial flutter in younger adults without structural disease | No comorbidities, normal echo, may be paroxysmal |

**Most common cause** — Hypertension and coronary artery disease account for ~60% of typical flutter.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria**
The definitive diagnosis of atrial flutter is made from a 12-lead ECG showing:
- Regular atrial activity at 250–350 bpm (typically ~300)
- Continuous undulating baseline with no isoelectric interval
- Sawtooth flutter waves best seen in II, III, aVF (negative in typical flutter)
- QRS narrow unless aberrancy; variable AV block

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **12-lead ECG** | Flutter waves, ventricular rate, block pattern | Confirm diagnosis, assess rate, exclude pre-excitation or infarction | Single finding that confirms: sawtooth waves |
| **Echocardiogram** | LV function, valve disease, atrial size, pericardial effusion | Assess structural substrate, guide management (e.g., cardioversion safety, anticoagulation) | Left atrial enlargement increases stroke risk; LV thrombus contraindicates cardioversion |
| **Thyroid function tests** | Elevated T4, low TSH | Rule out hyperthyroidism as reversible cause | If hyperthyroid → treat thyroid first |
| **Electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺)** | Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia | Correct triggers; low Mg²⁺ increases risk of digoxin toxicity | K⁺ >4.0, Mg²⁺ >0.8 mmol/L |
| **Chest X-ray** | Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, COPD changes | Assess for heart failure, lung disease | May show cardiomegaly or signs of underlying pulmonary cause |
| **Troponin** | Elevation if concurrent ACS | Exclude ACS if chest pain or hypotension | Flutter itself can cause demand ischemia but rarely raises troponin significantly |
| **Holter monitor** (if paroxysmal) | Captures episodes | If symptoms intermittent with normal ECG | Confirms diagnosis when ECG is non-diagnostic |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms?**  
The presence of characteristic sawtooth flutter waves on ECG with a regular atrial rate 250–350 bpm is diagnostic.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – to stabilize**

- **Hemodynamically unstable** (SBP <90, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, altered consciousness) → **synchronized cardioversion** (100–200 J biphasic). Why? The rapid ventricular rate is causing shock; electricity restores sinus rhythm fastest.
- **Hemodynamically stable** → **rate control** first. Slow AV conduction:
  - **Metoprolol** 2.5–5 mg IV over 2 min, repeat q5min up to 15 mg (or **esmolol** 500 mcg/kg bolus then 50 mcg/kg/min infusion)
  - If contraindicated (asthma, heart failure): **Diltiazem** 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 min, then 5–15 mg/hour infusion
  - Avoid **digoxin** in the acute setting unless heart failure with reduced EF — onset is slow, and ineffective in high sympathetic states.
- **Anticoagulation decision** — Calculate **CHA₂DS₂-VASc** score. If ≥2 (or ≥1 in women), start anticoagulation (e.g., apixaban 5 mg PO BID, or rivaroxaban 20 mg daily, or heparin bridge if temporary).
- **Correct triggers** — K⁺, Mg²⁺, thyroid status.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – to stabilize rhythm, plan long-term**

- If rate control is adequate (resting HR <100, symptoms resolved) → continue oral rate control (metoprolol tartrate 25–50 mg BID, or diltiazem CD 120–240 mg daily).
- If rate control fails or patient is young/symptomatic → consider **rhythm control**:
  - **Elective cardioversion** — requires TEE to exclude left atrial thrombus if flutter >48 hours or unknown duration. Energy 50–100 J biphasic (often lower than AF).
  - **Pharmacological cardioversion** — less effective than for AF. **Ibutilide** 1 mg IV over 10 min (repeat once if needed) can convert ~60% of typical flutter. Risk of torsades (monitor QT).
- **Ablation** — Gold standard for typical flutter. Catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus is curative in >90%. Offer to patients with recurrent symptomatic flutter.
- **Continuous telemetry** — watch for conversion to AF, or acceleration of ventricular rate after antiarrhythmic drugs.

**Long-term**

- **Lifelong anticoagulation** based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc (same as AF). Reassess periodically.
- **Rate control drugs** — if no ablation, maintain with beta-blocker or CCB.
- **Lifestyle modification** — treat underlying causes (hypertension, alcohol, sleep apnoea).
- **Follow-up** — repeat ECG at 1 month and 1 year; echocardiogram if new symptoms.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**

- Do not use **adenosine** to terminate flutter — it only blocks AV node, may cause prolonged asystole, does not terminate the circuit.
- Do not forget anticoagulation even if patient converts to sinus rhythm spontaneously — the stroke risk persists because the substrate remains.
- Do not cardiovert without TEE if flutter >48h duration — risk of embolic stroke is real.
- Do not stop beta-blocker abruptly if the flutter recurs — rebound tachycardia.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Atrial Flutter | Atrial Fibrillation | AVNRT (AV nodal reentrant tachycardia) | Sinus Tachycardia |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------------|
| Rhythm | Regular (irregular if variable block) | Irregularly irregular | Regular | Regular |
| Atrial rate | 250–350 bpm | >350 bpm (fine fibrillatory waves) | 140–250 bpm | 100–150 bpm (but can be higher) |
| P waves / baseline | Sawtooth flutter waves (II, III, aVF) | Chaotic, no P waves, coarse or fine fibrillation | Retrograde P waves (inverted in II, often hidden in QRS or at ST-segment) | Normal P waves (upright in II) |
| Onset / termination | Abrupt, may be paroxysmal or sustained | Abrupt or gradual | Abrupt, often triggered by PAC | Gradual, related to exertion/stress |
| Vagal manoeuvre | Increases AV block, reveals flutter | May slow ventricular rate briefly | May terminate abruptly (rarely) | Transient slowing, then returns |
| Carotid sinus massage effect | Shows flutter waves | Slows rate, no change in atrial activity | Terminates in ~50% of cases | Slight slowing |
| Common setting | Structural heart disease, post-op | Elderly, hypertension, HF | Young otherwise healthy adults | Infection, pain, hypovolemia, PE |
| Key differentiating clue | Sawtooth waves on ECG | Irregularly irregular pulse with absent P waves | Narrow QRS tachycardia with no P waves; adenosine terminates | P waves before QRS, rate <140 typically |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Stroke / systemic embolism**  
- How it presents: Sudden focal neurological deficit, or limb ischaemia, or mesenteric ischaemia.  
- Key signs: Atrial flutter is the likely source (left atrial appendage thrombus).  
- What to do: Immediate CT head, neurologist consult, anticoagulate (do not delay heparin even if CT pending).  

**Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy**  
- How it presents: Progressive dyspnoea, fatigue, oedema over weeks to months with persistently high ventricular rates (e.g., 1:1 or 2:1 block).  
- Key signs: Cardiomegaly on CXR, low LVEF on echo, absence of other cause.  
- What to do: Rate control (or rhythm control) → LV function often recovers within 3–6 months.  

**Myocardial ischaemia / infarction**  
- How it presents: Chest pain, troponin rise, ST changes on ECG (often demand ischaemia).  
- Key signs: Usually Type 2 MI (supply-demand mismatch).  
- What to do: Rate control first; if pain persists, coronary angiography may be needed.  

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”)**  
- A 78-year-old woman with known flutter who refuses anticoagulation presents with acute left-sided weakness and aphasia. ECG now shows sinus rhythm. The stroke is likely embolic. Missed anticoagulation → devastating outcome.  
- A 55-year-old man with untreated atrial flutter (rate ~150) for 3 months presents with NYHA III heart failure. Echo shows EF 25%. After rate control and cardioversion, EF improves to 45% in 4 months.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

1. **CHA₂DS₂-VASc** for stroke risk (remember: not just for AF):  
   - Congestive HF (1 point)  
   - Hypertension (1)  
   - Age ≥75 (2)  
   - Diabetes (1)  
   - Stroke/TIA/TE (2)  
   - Vascular disease (1)  
   - Age 65–74 (1)  
   - Sex category female (1)  

2. **FLUTTER** (to recall management steps):  
   - **F** – Find the flutter (confirm on ECG)  
   - **L** – Look for causes (echo, TSH, K+)  
   - **U** – Urgent rate control (beta-blocker or CCB)  
   - **T** – Think about anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)  
   - **T** – Terminate? (cardioversion vs ablation)  
   - **E** – Educate (lifestyle, follow-up)  
   – **R** – Recurrence risk (ablation is definitive)  

**High-yield clinical pearls**

1. **Sawtooth is best in II, III, aVF** — always scrutinize these leads. Flutter waves are typically negative in these leads (typical flutter).  
2. **2:1 AV block is the classic ratio** — ventricular rate is exactly or approximately half the atrial rate. Count the flutter waves between QRS complexes.  
3. **Carotid sinus massage is more useful than adenosine** — it is safer, cheaper, and reveals flutter waves. Adenosine can cause prolonged asystole in flutter.  
4. **Flutter rarely conducts 1:1** — if it does, think of AV nodal bypass (e.g., accessory pathway), or very high sympathetic tone (exercise, hyperthyroidism).  
5. **Anticoagulation decisions are identical to AF** — use the same risk stratification (CHA₂DS₂-VASc).  
6. **Catheter ablation is first-line for symptomatic typical flutter** — cure rate >90%, low risk. Do not offer lifelong drugs if patient is a candidate.  
7. **Digoxin is poor for rate control** — especially in high adrenergic states. Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine CCBs are preferred.  

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Mistaking atrial flutter for sinus tachycardia because you didn’t look for flutter waves.  
- Forgetting to calculate CHA₂DS₂-VASc in a patient with new-onset flutter.  
- Using digoxin as monotherapy for rate control in an acute setting.  
- Attempting cardioversion without ensuring anticoagulation adequacy.  
- Ignoring the possibility of variable block when the rhythm seems irregular.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior / ICU / crash team**

- **Hemodynamic instability** (SBP <90 mmHg, altered mental status, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, lactate >4 mmol/L) → call **crash team** for urgent cardioversion.  
- **Ventricular rate >200 bpm** and unstable → likely 1:1 conduction; risk of ventricular fibrillation. Immediate senior review and cardioversion.  
- **Syncope or near-syncope** during arrhythmia → may indicate very rapid rate, or conversion to AF with fast ventricular response.  
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** (especially RR >24, SpO₂ <91% on air, HR >130, SBP <90, or any new confusion) → escalate to medical registrar / critical care outreach.  
- **New tachyarrhythmia in the setting of acute coronary syndrome** (ongoing chest pain, ST elevation) → urgent cardiology review; consider ICU for rhythm control.  
- **Failure of rate control** after 30–60 minutes (HR >130 despite two doses of IV beta-blocker) → senior cardiology advice; may need IV amiodarone or cardioversion.  
- **Any suspicion of stroke** (sudden neurological deficit) → immediate stroke team, CT head, and do not delay anticoagulation if no haemorrhage.  

**Specific thresholds**

| Parameter | Action threshold |
|-----------|------------------|
| Heart rate | >150 despite initial therapy |
| Systolic BP | <90 mmHg (or >40 mmHg drop from baseline) |
| GCS | <15 (new confusion) |
| Lactate | >2.5 mmol/L (indicative of tissue hypoperfusion) |
| SpO₂ | <92% on room air (consider pulmonary oedema) |
| Urine output | <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 2 consecutive hours |

rawMarkdown:
## Atrial Flutter

**One-liner:** A supraventricular macro-reentrant tachycardia with a characteristic “sawtooth” atrial rhythm (~300 bpm), causing symptoms and stroke risk, especially in older adults with structural heart disease.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You’ll See

**Classic vignette**
You are called to see a 72-year-old man with known hypertension and mild COPD. He is in the surgical ward post-hip replacement. The nurses charted his pulse at 135 bpm, BP 105/60, SpO₂ 96% on air. He feels “fluttering in my chest” and slightly short of breath. ECG shows narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm with no clear P waves — but on lead II you spot a subtle “sawtooth” pattern. After carotid sinus massage (or adenosine 6 mg IV) the ventricular rate slows transiently and the sawtooth waves become obvious.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues and what they tell you**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Age >65, hypertension, COPD | Structural heart disease and atrial stretch predispose to reentry circuits |
| Post-operative setting | Increased sympathetic tone, fluid shifts, electrolyte changes trigger arrhythmia |
| Heart rate 135–150 bpm | This is around 2:1 AV block — typical flutter rate ~300, so ventricular rate is half |
| Narrow QRS tachycardia | Supraventricular origin; broad QRS would suggest VT or pre-excited |
| Sawtooth waves on lead II / III / aVF | The classic “flutter waves” — continuous, biphasic, best seen in inferior leads |
| Carotid sinus massage / adenosine transiently slows rate | AV nodal blockade reveals underlying flutter waves without terminating the arrhythmia |
| Mild SOB, palpitations, borderline BP | Hemodynamic compromise is often less severe than with AF at same ventricular rate, but can be significant |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**

- **Variable AV block** — The patient may have an irregularly irregular pulse (like AF) if the block changes from 2:1 to 3:1 or 4:1. Don’t be fooled: look for flutter waves buried in the T waves.
- **Broad QRS tachycardia** — If pre-existing bundle branch block or rate-related aberrancy, the QRS can be wide. Mistaking this for ventricular tachycardia is a classic error. Clues: irregularity, sawtooth waves, adenosine response.
- **Silent / isolated stroke** — The first manifestation may be an embolic stroke. ECG shows flutter in sinus rhythm? No — you must maintain high suspicion in any elderly stroke patient. Ask about palpitations.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition**
Atrial flutter is a fast, regular rhythm originating in the atria due to a single macro-reentrant circuit. The atria beat at about 300 times per minute, but the AV node can only pass through a fraction of those impulses, typically every second (2:1 block) or third (3:1 block), giving a ventricular rate of 150 or 100 bpm.

**Why it matters clinically**

- **Frequency** — Less common than AF (about 1/10th as common) but still encountered regularly in acute medicine, cardiology, and perioperative care.
- **Stakes** — Atrial flutter carries a stroke risk almost as high as AF (though some studies suggest slightly lower). Anticoagulation is mandatory for most patients.
- **Pitfalls** — Easily missed on ECG if you don’t look for sawtooth waves. Easily treated but often recurs without definitive therapy (ablation). Mismanagement of ventricular rate can cause heart failure.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story**
Atrial flutter is a reentrant tachycardia. In typical (isthmus-dependent) flutter, the circuit runs around the tricuspid annulus, using the cavotricuspid isthmus as a critical slow conduction zone. The wavefront travels up the right atrial free wall, down the interatrial septum, and back through the isthmus. This loop takes about 200 ms, giving an atrial cycle length of ~200 ms → atrial rate ~300/min.

**Why each key sign happens**

- **Sawtooth waves on ECG** — The continuous, undulating baseline (best seen in II, III, aVF) reflects atrial depolarisation without an isoelectric interval. The circuit produces a constant direction of activation that creates biphasic deflections.
- **Ventricular rate ~150 bpm** — The AV node cannot conduct 1:1 at 300 bpm (its effective refractory period is too long). In a healthy heart, 2:1 block is the default. Higher degrees (3:1, 4:1) occur with AV nodal disease or drugs.
- **Irregular pulse if variable block** — Changes in autonomic tone (breathing, walking, pain) alter AV node refractoriness, causing the block ratio to vary and making the rhythm irregular.
- **Carotid sinus massage shows flutter** — Vagal manoeuvres prolong AV nodal refractoriness, increasing block to 3:1 or 4:1 without affecting the atrial circuit. The sawtooth becomes visible because fewer QRS complexes obscure the baseline.
- **Adenosine transiently slows but does not terminate** — Adenosine blocks AV node conduction but the atrial reentry circuit is not dependent on AV node. After adenosine wears off (seconds), the atrial rate returns. This distinguishes flutter from AVNRT.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern recognition**
The post-operative patient has a hyperadrenergic state → decreased AV node refractoriness → 2:1 block → ventricular rate 150. Baseline structural changes (atrial stretch from hypertension) maintain the substrate for reentry.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Structural heart disease** (most common) | Hypertension, coronary artery disease, valvular heart disease (especially mitral valve), cardiomyopathy | Older patient with known IHD or HF; left atrial enlargement on echo |
| **Post-cardiac surgery** | Especially after CABG or valve surgery — atrial incisions or ischemia create substrate | Recent sternotomy, drains, chest tubes |
| **Pulmonary causes** | COPD, pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, sleep apnoea | Hypoxia, CO₂ retention, right heart strain |
| **Endocrine / metabolic** | Hyperthyroidism (especially in elderly), electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) | Weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance; or diuretic use, low K⁺ |
| **Toxic / drug-induced** | Alcohol (“holiday heart”), caffeine, sympathomimetics, digoxin toxicity (less common) | Binge drinking, new stimulant medication |
| **Idiopathic** | Lone atrial flutter in younger adults without structural disease | No comorbidities, normal echo, may be paroxysmal |

**Most common cause** — Hypertension and coronary artery disease account for ~60% of typical flutter.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria**
The definitive diagnosis of atrial flutter is made from a 12-lead ECG showing:
- Regular atrial activity at 250–350 bpm (typically ~300)
- Continuous undulating baseline with no isoelectric interval
- Sawtooth flutter waves best seen in II, III, aVF (negative in typical flutter)
- QRS narrow unless aberrancy; variable AV block

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **12-lead ECG** | Flutter waves, ventricular rate, block pattern | Confirm diagnosis, assess rate, exclude pre-excitation or infarction | Single finding that confirms: sawtooth waves |
| **Echocardiogram** | LV function, valve disease, atrial size, pericardial effusion | Assess structural substrate, guide management (e.g., cardioversion safety, anticoagulation) | Left atrial enlargement increases stroke risk; LV thrombus contraindicates cardioversion |
| **Thyroid function tests** | Elevated T4, low TSH | Rule out hyperthyroidism as reversible cause | If hyperthyroid → treat thyroid first |
| **Electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺)** | Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia | Correct triggers; low Mg²⁺ increases risk of digoxin toxicity | K⁺ >4.0, Mg²⁺ >0.8 mmol/L |
| **Chest X-ray** | Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion, COPD changes | Assess for heart failure, lung disease | May show cardiomegaly or signs of underlying pulmonary cause |
| **Troponin** | Elevation if concurrent ACS | Exclude ACS if chest pain or hypotension | Flutter itself can cause demand ischemia but rarely raises troponin significantly |
| **Holter monitor** (if paroxysmal) | Captures episodes | If symptoms intermittent with normal ECG | Confirms diagnosis when ECG is non-diagnostic |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms?**  
The presence of characteristic sawtooth flutter waves on ECG with a regular atrial rate 250–350 bpm is diagnostic.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – to stabilize**

- **Hemodynamically unstable** (SBP <90, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, altered consciousness) → **synchronized cardioversion** (100–200 J biphasic). Why? The rapid ventricular rate is causing shock; electricity restores sinus rhythm fastest.
- **Hemodynamically stable** → **rate control** first. Slow AV conduction:
  - **Metoprolol** 2.5–5 mg IV over 2 min, repeat q5min up to 15 mg (or **esmolol** 500 mcg/kg bolus then 50 mcg/kg/min infusion)
  - If contraindicated (asthma, heart failure): **Diltiazem** 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 min, then 5–15 mg/hour infusion
  - Avoid **digoxin** in the acute setting unless heart failure with reduced EF — onset is slow, and ineffective in high sympathetic states.
- **Anticoagulation decision** — Calculate **CHA₂DS₂-VASc** score. If ≥2 (or ≥1 in women), start anticoagulation (e.g., apixaban 5 mg PO BID, or rivaroxaban 20 mg daily, or heparin bridge if temporary).
- **Correct triggers** — K⁺, Mg²⁺, thyroid status.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – to stabilize rhythm, plan long-term**

- If rate control is adequate (resting HR <100, symptoms resolved) → continue oral rate control (metoprolol tartrate 25–50 mg BID, or diltiazem CD 120–240 mg daily).
- If rate control fails or patient is young/symptomatic → consider **rhythm control**:
  - **Elective cardioversion** — requires TEE to exclude left atrial thrombus if flutter >48 hours or unknown duration. Energy 50–100 J biphasic (often lower than AF).
  - **Pharmacological cardioversion** — less effective than for AF. **Ibutilide** 1 mg IV over 10 min (repeat once if needed) can convert ~60% of typical flutter. Risk of torsades (monitor QT).
- **Ablation** — Gold standard for typical flutter. Catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus is curative in >90%. Offer to patients with recurrent symptomatic flutter.
- **Continuous telemetry** — watch for conversion to AF, or acceleration of ventricular rate after antiarrhythmic drugs.

**Long-term**

- **Lifelong anticoagulation** based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc (same as AF). Reassess periodically.
- **Rate control drugs** — if no ablation, maintain with beta-blocker or CCB.
- **Lifestyle modification** — treat underlying causes (hypertension, alcohol, sleep apnoea).
- **Follow-up** — repeat ECG at 1 month and 1 year; echocardiogram if new symptoms.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls)**

- Do not use **adenosine** to terminate flutter — it only blocks AV node, may cause prolonged asystole, does not terminate the circuit.
- Do not forget anticoagulation even if patient converts to sinus rhythm spontaneously — the stroke risk persists because the substrate remains.
- Do not cardiovert without TEE if flutter >48h duration — risk of embolic stroke is real.
- Do not stop beta-blocker abruptly if the flutter recurs — rebound tachycardia.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Atrial Flutter | Atrial Fibrillation | AVNRT (AV nodal reentrant tachycardia) | Sinus Tachycardia |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------------|
| Rhythm | Regular (irregular if variable block) | Irregularly irregular | Regular | Regular |
| Atrial rate | 250–350 bpm | >350 bpm (fine fibrillatory waves) | 140–250 bpm | 100–150 bpm (but can be higher) |
| P waves / baseline | Sawtooth flutter waves (II, III, aVF) | Chaotic, no P waves, coarse or fine fibrillation | Retrograde P waves (inverted in II, often hidden in QRS or at ST-segment) | Normal P waves (upright in II) |
| Onset / termination | Abrupt, may be paroxysmal or sustained | Abrupt or gradual | Abrupt, often triggered by PAC | Gradual, related to exertion/stress |
| Vagal manoeuvre | Increases AV block, reveals flutter | May slow ventricular rate briefly | May terminate abruptly (rarely) | Transient slowing, then returns |
| Carotid sinus massage effect | Shows flutter waves | Slows rate, no change in atrial activity | Terminates in ~50% of cases | Slight slowing |
| Common setting | Structural heart disease, post-op | Elderly, hypertension, HF | Young otherwise healthy adults | Infection, pain, hypovolemia, PE |
| Key differentiating clue | Sawtooth waves on ECG | Irregularly irregular pulse with absent P waves | Narrow QRS tachycardia with no P waves; adenosine terminates | P waves before QRS, rate <140 typically |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Stroke / systemic embolism**  
- How it presents: Sudden focal neurological deficit, or limb ischaemia, or mesenteric ischaemia.  
- Key signs: Atrial flutter is the likely source (left atrial appendage thrombus).  
- What to do: Immediate CT head, neurologist consult, anticoagulate (do not delay heparin even if CT pending).  

**Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy**  
- How it presents: Progressive dyspnoea, fatigue, oedema over weeks to months with persistently high ventricular rates (e.g., 1:1 or 2:1 block).  
- Key signs: Cardiomegaly on CXR, low LVEF on echo, absence of other cause.  
- What to do: Rate control (or rhythm control) → LV function often recovers within 3–6 months.  

**Myocardial ischaemia / infarction**  
- How it presents: Chest pain, troponin rise, ST changes on ECG (often demand ischaemia).  
- Key signs: Usually Type 2 MI (supply-demand mismatch).  
- What to do: Rate control first; if pain persists, coronary angiography may be needed.  

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”)**  
- A 78-year-old woman with known flutter who refuses anticoagulation presents with acute left-sided weakness and aphasia. ECG now shows sinus rhythm. The stroke is likely embolic. Missed anticoagulation → devastating outcome.  
- A 55-year-old man with untreated atrial flutter (rate ~150) for 3 months presents with NYHA III heart failure. Echo shows EF 25%. After rate control and cardioversion, EF improves to 45% in 4 months.  

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**

1. **CHA₂DS₂-VASc** for stroke risk (remember: not just for AF):  
   - Congestive HF (1 point)  
   - Hypertension (1)  
   - Age ≥75 (2)  
   - Diabetes (1)  
   - Stroke/TIA/TE (2)  
   - Vascular disease (1)  
   - Age 65–74 (1)  
   - Sex category female (1)  

2. **FLUTTER** (to recall management steps):  
   - **F** – Find the flutter (confirm on ECG)  
   - **L** – Look for causes (echo, TSH, K+)  
   - **U** – Urgent rate control (beta-blocker or CCB)  
   - **T** – Think about anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)  
   - **T** – Terminate? (cardioversion vs ablation)  
   - **E** – Educate (lifestyle, follow-up)  
   – **R** – Recurrence risk (ablation is definitive)  

**High-yield clinical pearls**

1. **Sawtooth is best in II, III, aVF** — always scrutinize these leads. Flutter waves are typically negative in these leads (typical flutter).  
2. **2:1 AV block is the classic ratio** — ventricular rate is exactly or approximately half the atrial rate. Count the flutter waves between QRS complexes.  
3. **Carotid sinus massage is more useful than adenosine** — it is safer, cheaper, and reveals flutter waves. Adenosine can cause prolonged asystole in flutter.  
4. **Flutter rarely conducts 1:1** — if it does, think of AV nodal bypass (e.g., accessory pathway), or very high sympathetic tone (exercise, hyperthyroidism).  
5. **Anticoagulation decisions are identical to AF** — use the same risk stratification (CHA₂DS₂-VASc).  
6. **Catheter ablation is first-line for symptomatic typical flutter** — cure rate >90%, low risk. Do not offer lifelong drugs if patient is a candidate.  
7. **Digoxin is poor for rate control** — especially in high adrenergic states. Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine CCBs are preferred.  

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Mistaking atrial flutter for sinus tachycardia because you didn’t look for flutter waves.  
- Forgetting to calculate CHA₂DS₂-VASc in a patient with new-onset flutter.  
- Using digoxin as monotherapy for rate control in an acute setting.  
- Attempting cardioversion without ensuring anticoagulation adequacy.  
- Ignoring the possibility of variable block when the rhythm seems irregular.  

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior / ICU / crash team**

- **Hemodynamic instability** (SBP <90 mmHg, altered mental status, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, lactate >4 mmol/L) → call **crash team** for urgent cardioversion.  
- **Ventricular rate >200 bpm** and unstable → likely 1:1 conduction; risk of ventricular fibrillation. Immediate senior review and cardioversion.  
- **Syncope or near-syncope** during arrhythmia → may indicate very rapid rate, or conversion to AF with fast ventricular response.  
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** (especially RR >24, SpO₂ <91% on air, HR >130, SBP <90, or any new confusion) → escalate to medical registrar / critical care outreach.  
- **New tachyarrhythmia in the setting of acute coronary syndrome** (ongoing chest pain, ST elevation) → urgent cardiology review; consider ICU for rhythm control.  
- **Failure of rate control** after 30–60 minutes (HR >130 despite two doses of IV beta-blocker) → senior cardiology advice; may need IV amiodarone or cardioversion.  
- **Any suspicion of stroke** (sudden neurological deficit) → immediate stroke team, CT head, and do not delay anticoagulation if no haemorrhage.  

**Specific thresholds**

| Parameter | Action threshold |
|-----------|------------------|
| Heart rate | >150 despite initial therapy |
| Systolic BP | <90 mmHg (or >40 mmHg drop from baseline) |
| GCS | <15 (new confusion) |
| Lactate | >2.5 mmol/L (indicative of tissue hypoperfusion) |
| SpO₂ | <92% on room air (consider pulmonary oedema) |
| Urine output | <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 2 consecutive hours |
